corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: One can see that CatBoost outperforms other algorithms on all the considered datasets.
To demonstrate that our implementation of plain boosting is an appropriate baseline for our research, we show that a raw setting of CatBoost provides state-of-the-art quality.
Note that the ordered TS used in CatBoost significantly outperform all other approaches.
Also note that computational complexity of this algorithm exceeds one of classic GBDT by factor of K.
In this section, we formally describe the CatBoost algorithm introduced in Section 5.
In Algorithm 3, we provide more information on particular details including the speeding up trick introduced in paragraph "Complexity".
The key step of the CatBoost algorithm is the procedure of building a tree described in detail in Function BuildT ree.
To obtain the formal description of the CatBoost algorithm without the speeding up trick, one should replace log 2 n by n in line 6 of Algorithm 3 and use Algorithm 2 instead of Function BuildT ree.
Using ApplyM ode instead of a permutation in function GetLeaf in line 15 of Algorithm 3 means that we use TS calculated on the whole training data to apply the trained model on a new example.

If the same dataset D = D 1 = D 2 is used in Equation (6) for both h 1 and h 2 , then This theorem means that the trained model is an unbiased estimate of the true dependence y = f * (x), when we use independent datasets at each gradient step.
On the other hand, if we use the same dataset at each step, we suffer from a bias , which is inversely proportional to the data size n.

The detailed description of the experimental setup together with dataset descriptions is available in Appendix D.

We also measured statistical significance of improvements presented in Table : except three datasets (Appetency, Churn and Upselling) the improvements are statistically significant with p-value 0.01 measured by the paired one-tailed t-test.
We also empirically analyzed the running times of the algorithms on Epsilon dataset.
Train-test splits Each dataset was randomly split into training set (80%) and test set (20%).

For each pair of datasets (D train i , D val i ), i = 1, . . . , 5, and (D f ull_train , D test ), we preprocess the categorical features by calculating ordered TS (described in Section 3.2) on the basis of a random permutation of the examples of the first (training) dataset.

Two critical algorithmic advances introduced in CatBoost are the implementation of ordered boosting, a permutation-driven alternative to the classic algorithm, and an innovative algorithm for processing categorical features.
Both techniques were created to fight a prediction shift caused by a special kind of target leakage present in all currently existing implementations of gradient boosting algorithms.
Gradient boosting is a powerful machine-learning technique that achieves state-of-the-art results in a variety of practical tasks.
Gradient boosting is a powerful machine-learning technique that achieves state-of-the-art results in a variety of practical tasks.

Relying on it, we derive ordered boosting, a modification of standard gradient boosting algorithm, which avoids target leakage (Section 4), and a new algorithm for processing categorical features (Section 3).

Their combination is implemented as an open-source library 1 called CatBoost (for "Categorical Boosting"), which outperforms the existing state-of-the-art implementations of gradient boosted decision trees -XGBoost and LightGBM -on a diverse set of popular machine learning tasks (see Section 6).

downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
